title: Combinatorics of Symbol Alphabets in N=4 Yang-Mills
speaker: Anastasia Volovich

proof_stages.label: Amplitudes, kinematics, and symbols
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames planar N=4 Yang-Mills amplitudes as functions indexed by particle number, helicity sector, and loop order on Gr(4,n), and defines polylogarithmic symbols and symbol alphabets as records of the functions' singularity data.

proof_stages.label: Known low-point symbol alphabets
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the computed alphabets at six, seven, eight, and nine points, emphasizing that Plücker-coordinate alphabets at six and seven points give way to more complicated rational and algebraic square-root letters at eight and nine points.

proof_stages.label: Cluster-algebra interpretation and infinite-mutation algebraic letters
proof_stages.summary: The six- and seven-point alphabets are identified with cluster coordinates of the finite Grassmannian cluster algebras Gr(4,6) and Gr(4,7), while the infinite cluster algebras beginning at Gr(4,8) explain algebraic square-root letters through limits of infinite mutation sequences.

proof_stages.label: Tensor-diagram packaging of alphabet letters
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces SL_k tensor diagrams/webs and uses their relation to cluster variables to repackage the long rational and algebraic symbol-letter lists, especially through certain web types up to nine points.

proof_stages.label: From alphabets to words and functions
proof_stages.summary: Beyond the set of letters, amplitudes satisfy cluster adjacency constraints on consecutive symbol entries, and the speaker points to cluster polylogarithms as a hoped-for function-level refinement of this cluster structure.

proof_stages.label: Physical computation and bootstrap origin of the data
proof_stages.summary: The known alphabets and amplitudes are traced to explicit two-loop computations, the Q-bar recursion to higher points, and the Dixon bootstrap program, which starts from an alphabet ansatz and imposes physical and mathematical constraints to determine high-loop amplitudes.

proof_stages.label: Higher-loop completeness via Landau analysis
proof_stages.summary: To justify that no six- or seven-point letters are missing at higher loops, the speaker proposes analyzing Landau equations through reduced Ziggurat graphs, notes the appearance of candidate extra letters, and reports bootstrap evidence that such extra letters have zero coefficient.

proof_stages.label: Higher-point and elliptic frontiers
proof_stages.summary: For higher multiplicity, recent evaluations of all two-loop integrals and related elliptic-polylogarithmic symbol technology provide new data whose letters and functions must be analyzed, with one-loop n-gon formulas and elliptic methods presented as key tools for the next stage.

examples.name: Dilogarithm symbol identity
examples.note: The standard dilogarithm identity involving Li_2(x), Li_2(-x), and Li_2(x^2) is used to show concretely how the symbol turns polylogarithm identities into tensor algebra.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the symbols of both sides of the dilogarithm identity and manipulates the tensor factors to verify equality

passes.reason: A standard dilogarithm identity is used only to illustrate how symbols simplify polylogarithm relations.

examples.name: The A2 cluster algebra
examples.note: The two-node A2 quiver is used as a small refresher example before introducing the Grassmannian cluster algebras relevant to six- and seven-point alphabets.
examples.engagement_reason: the A2 cluster algebra is used as a named introductory example of a quiver with a mutation rule, without carrying out its mutation sequence or computing its variables

passes.reason: The A2 cluster algebra is a standard finite-type cluster algebra used as an introductory example of mutation.
The rank-two finite-type A2 cluster algebra is the smallest nontrivial mutation toy model used for exposition.
The A2 cluster algebra is a standard finite-type cluster algebra used pedagogically, not a sporadic or extremal object.
The type A2 cluster algebra is a standard finite Dynkin-family example used to introduce mutation.
The A2 cluster algebra is a finite-type but systematic member of the type-A family used as an introductory example.
The A2 cluster algebra is an ordinary finite-type member of the infinite A-family used as a basic example of mutations.
Used as a basic illustrative cluster-algebra example, structurally the minimal nontrivial finite-type rank-two case.
used as the basic rank-two toy model of cluster mutation, essentially a minimal nontrivial cluster algebra rather than an adversarial case

examples.name: Tensor diagram for a Plücker coordinate
examples.note: A simple SL_k tensor diagram whose invariant is the Plücker coordinate <1234> makes the tensor-diagram dictionary visible.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the tensor-diagram-to-invariant association to a displayed diagram and identifies the resulting invariant as the Plücker coordinate <1234>

examples.name: Massless box Landau singularity
examples.note: The massless box integral is used as the simplest Feynman-integral instance where solving Landau equations produces a branch singularity.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies Landau equations to the massless box integral and says solving them gives its singularity

passes.reason: The massless box Landau singularity is used as a standard simple illustration of solving Landau equations.
The massless one-loop box is a limiting minimal integral and its Landau solution is a critical boundary singularity.
The massless box is a zero-mass limiting case used to illustrate Landau-equation singularities.
The massless box is a zero-mass limiting four-point box, and its Landau solution is a critical branch-locus example.
The massless box is a zero-mass, minimal one-loop limiting case used to demonstrate Landau singularities.
A Landau singularity of the massless box is a critical boundary locus of the integral, used as a simple demonstration of Landau equations.
Used to illustrate Landau equations, and a massless-box Landau singularity is a critical boundary point in a degenerate massless limit.

examples.name: Resistor-network Y-Delta analogy
